mnemonics.title: M F C C Melody
mnemonics.content: M-Mel scale filters, F-Frequency analysis, C-Cepstral coefficients, C-Compress dynamics. Remember: 'Mel Filters Capture Sound's Character' — helps recall that MFCC extracts the most important acoustic features from speech waveforms.

mnemonics.title: H M M Hidden States
mnemonics.content: Hidden Markov Models: 'Happy Mice Make Patterns' — Hidden states (mice) generate observable outputs (cheese trails), and we infer the hidden states from what we observe. In speech: hidden phoneme states produce observed audio features.

mnemonics.title: C T C Connection Timing
mnemonics.content: CTC: 'Connect Time Characters' — Connects input audio frames to output characters without strict alignment. Remember: 'Can't Tell Characters' because CTC handles variable-length inputs and outputs by inserting blanks.

mnemonics.title: A S R Auto Speech Flow
mnemonics.content: ASR Pipeline: 'Audio Goes Through Feature Extraction → Acoustic Model → Language Model → Decoder'. Remember: 'Analogy: Sound Travels Efficiently And Gets Decoded' — A-S-A-R in order.

mnemonics.title: Wav2Vec Wave to Vector
mnemonics.content: Wav2Vec: 'Waveforms Converted To Vector Representations' — Self-supervised learning that learns speech representations from raw audio. Remember: 'Wav2Vec Vastly Learns Vocal Embeddings' — uses contrastive learning.

acronyms.usage: Used in feature extraction stage to represent the spectral envelope of speech. Apply when preprocessing raw audio into compact feature vectors for model input.
The overall system that converts spoken language to text. Use when referring to the complete speech-to-text pipeline.
A loss function used for sequence labeling where input and output lengths differ. Apply when training end-to-end models without explicit alignment.
Statistical model assuming hidden states generating observations. Used in traditional acoustic modeling before deep learning era.

acronyms.expansion: Deep Neural Network - Hidden Markov Model
acronyms.usage: Hybrid architecture where DNN replaces emission probabilities in HMM. The standard deep learning approach before end-to-end models.
Metric: (Substitutions + Deletions + Insertions) / Total Words × 100%. Use to evaluate ASR system performance — lower is better.
End-to-end model that jointly models acoustic and language information. Use when studying streaming speech recognition architectures.
Models that directly map audio to text without separate components. Use when referring to modern architectures like Deep Speech, Wav2Vec, etc.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a musical score where time flows horizontally (left to right) and pitch flows vertically (low to high). Bright colors = loud sounds. Speech appears as horizontal bands at different heights — vowels are steady low-frequency bands, consonants are brief high-frequency bursts. Picture: 'sheet music of speech'.

Visualize triangular filters stacked on top of each other along the frequency axis, getting wider as frequency increases. Like a funnel shape — narrow at low frequencies (detailed), wide at high frequencies (coarse). This mimics human ear perception: we hear low frequencies more precisely.

Imagine a tennis ball bouncing along a timeline — the ball represents the output, and blank spaces are the bounces between letters. CTC inserts blanks '_' to collapse repeated characters. Picture: 'Maaary had a little lamb' → 'M_a_ry_had_a_little_lamb' in alignment.

Picture a spotlight scanning over the spectrogram (encoder output) while the decoder generates each output character. The spotlight focuses on relevant audio segments for current prediction — brighter spotlight = stronger attention. Like following a speaker's mouth movements while listening.

Think of a label maker that reads audio 'barcodes' (features) and outputs phonetic labels. The acoustic model maps acoustic features to phoneme probabilities. Picture: 'sound translator' converting audio patterns to language units.

Imagine a wise elder suggesting probable next words based on context. Given 'The cat sat on the...', the language model boosts probability of 'mat' and reduces 'football'. Picture: 'probability predictor' for word sequences.

analogies.analogy: Like taking a detailed photograph and compressing it into a short written description. The original audio (photo) has too much data; MFCC extracts only the essential characteristics — like noting 'red Ferrari' instead of every pixel. This compact representation captures what matters for understanding speech.

Like transcribing a continuous spoken sentence without knowing where each word starts. If someone says 'helloworld' quickly, CTC figures out the boundaries and outputs 'hello world' by learning that certain patterns should be collapsed. Similar to a secretary who fills in missing punctuation from fast speech.

Like a simultaneous interpreter at a UN meeting. The encoder (listening to source language) processes the full speech, and the decoder (translating) doesn't just look at everything — it focuses on relevant parts via attention, like the interpreter focusing on current speaker's mouth and gestures.

Like autocorrect on your phone. When you type 'their going to', autocorrect suggests 'they're going to' based on grammar rules. Similarly, the language model guides the acoustic output toward grammatically correct and contextually appropriate text.

Like identifying individual musical notes from a song recording. Just as notes combine to make music, phonemes combine to form words. The acoustic model learns to recognize these fundamental 'sound notes' that make up all spoken language.

Like learning a language by listening to thousands of hours of radio without any subtitles. The model discovers patterns in speech sounds on its own (contrastive learning), similar to how infants learn language by hearing before they understand words.
